"$ANET Fundamental Deep Dive 🟢 Arista Networks

OVERVIEW

Arista Networks operates as a leading provider of networking solutions across AI/HPC infrastructure and routing environments. FY2025 earnings showed significant momentum, with annual revenue of $9B, growing +29% YoY and guiding strongly. Hyperscaler demand and Ethernet fabric expansion delivered the bulk of growth, supported by 400G/800G switches scaling into higher-speed AI clusters. This is only the start of this vertical and will drive growth for years. Excited to see how their 1.6T+ programs and continued AI ramps perform in hyperscaler data centers in 2H 2026 and beyond.

Arista provides the full stack, providing high-performance Ethernet switches with EOS software. This allows ease of virtualization. This company is at the forefront of converting silicon into deployable, rack-ready systems at volume for hyperscalers. They're the pure-play leader enforcing the 1.6T+ photonics wave. It's a significant reason for my comfort in investing in the company as a leveraged AI networking play.

The previous earnings calls confirmed 1.6T+ data center switches are now ready to order, with XPO liquid-cooled pluggable optic modules delivering up to 12.8 Tbps per module. This advancement delivers massive scale and efficiency for hyperscaler AI clusters, addressing power and density limits in next-gen fabrics. The campus and routing segments provide more stable contributions, though with focus shifting toward high-margin AI data center opportunities.

Profitability is set to explode in the years moving forward as AI products mix deeper into the portfolio. Non-GAAP operating margin reached new highs around 47%+ with strong EPS growth. FCF remains exceptional with minimal capital intensity. Competition is present with $NVDA as the behemoth expands into the space with Spectrum-X; however, demand is rampant and Arista is the reliable, proven leader that networking engineers love according to feedback.

------------------------------------

GROWTH OUTLOOK

$ANET sits at a $220 Billion valuation. Its valuation reflects strong growth expectations with a ~60x Trailing P/E and ~24x P/S Ratio, indicative of the stock sitting at a premium. In my eyes, it's entirely deserved and can easily expand considering it's a extremely high margin business. Leveraging their +25-30% sequential YoY growth, it present a unique opportunity as a pure-play "picks-and-shovels" company.

Arista Networks has consistently outperformed expectations. Management projects robust revenue growth over the next 3–5 years, driven largely by AI data center + Ethernet acceleration. Anticipate a smooth rise in valuation. Their TAM is significant and growing exponentially. It's an extremely comfortable long moving forward. Product roadmaps support this outlook, as shown by their unveiling of 1.6T+ systems and XPO liquid-cooled modules set to hit the the income statement 2H 2026 onwards.

Over FY2026, the company is projected to report ~$11.5B Revenue and $3.60 adjusted EPS annually. This will be followed by even stronger growth in 2027, implying visibility and penetration improvement.

Risks include cyclical demand, supply chain execution, customer concentration, and broader macro slowdowns in tech spending.

------------------------------------

FAIR VALUATION ESTIMATE

Using an aggregated/blended valuation approach based on many different disciplines of FA (DCF, Relative Multiples, Analysts, etc.),

Current FVE = $240/share

This does not take into account its future prospects and potential for technical momentum to accelerate price far beyond this FVE.

------------------------------------

LONG-TERM ESTIMATES

Assuming $ANET consistently meets aggressive growth targets (revenue ramp achieved with margins maintained), this is their potential path forward valuation-wise when using P/E multiples and expected EPS values.

EY2026 = $300+
EY2027 = $450+
EY2028 = $600+
EY2029 = $800+

All in all, another high conviction investment that can multiply its valuation safely and securely over the next half a decade."}}}, "rest_id": "2062265269427552310", "source": "<a href="https://mobile.twitter.com" rel="nofollow">Twitter Web App</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "2066", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
🚨 Photonics may become the most important technology shift of the next decade.

Not AI software.

Not apps.

Not even GPUs alone.

The REAL revolution may be the replacement of electricity with LIGHT inside computing infrastructure. 🧵

Silicon electronics are approaching physical limits.

Modern AI infrastructure now faces: ⚠️ bandwidth ceilings ⚠️ power-consumption explosions ⚠️ heat-density problems ⚠️ latency bottlenecks ⚠️ interconnect saturation

The current architecture is becoming unsustainable.

This is where photonics enters.

Photonics = using LIGHT (photons) instead of electricity (electrons) to: 💡 transmit data 💡 process signals 💡 move information 💡 scale compute infrastructure

The implications are enormous.

We believe photonics is no longer speculative science fiction.

It is increasingly becoming: ⚠️ necessary ⚠️ commercially viable ⚠️ strategically essential

Especially for hyperscale AI systems.

The AI boom itself is creating the problem photonics solves.

As models scale: 📈 compute demand explodes 📈 rack density rises 📈 cooling loads surge 📈 electricity consumption skyrockets

Copper interconnects increasingly become the bottleneck.

Traditional electrical systems waste massive amounts of energy as heat.

Photonics changes the equation: ⚡ lower power consumption ⚡ faster transmission ⚡ lower latency ⚡ higher bandwidth ⚡ less thermal stress

This is why hyperscalers are aggressively pursuing it.

In this case the sequence is straightforward:

Step 1: 🤖 AI models scale exponentially

Step 2: ⚠️ Electrical interconnects hit limits

Step 3: 🔥 Cooling and power costs explode

Step 4: 💡 Photonics becomes economically necessary

Step 5: 🏗 Entire data-center architecture changes

This transition is already happening quietly.

Over the next 3–5 years: ⚠️ co-packaged optics ⚠️ optical switching ⚠️ silicon photonics ⚠️ photonic networking

…will increasingly replace traditional copper infrastructure.

The implications go far beyond performance.

Photonics may fundamentally reshape: 🏭 semiconductor design ⚡ power consumption 💧 cooling infrastructure 🧠 AI deployment economics 🏢 hyperscale construction

We believe this becomes one of the clearest examples of K-shaped technological consolidation.

Why?

Because only the largest players can afford: 💰 photonic R&D 💰 custom AI infrastructure 💰 advanced packaging 💰 optical integration (Watch chip makers rotating, new partnerships)

Smaller competitors increasingly get locked out.

The photonics transition favors: ✅ hyperscalers ✅ mega-cap semiconductor firms ✅ defense-linked infrastructure players ✅ vertically integrated ecosystems

The technological moat widens.

🚨 INVESTABLE PLAYERS

Several companies already sit directly inside this transition.

⚡ Broadcom ($AVGO) ⚡ Coherent ($COHR) ⚡ Lumentum ($LITE) ⚡ Intel ($INTC) ⚡ Laser Photonics ($LASE)

Each attacks a different layer of the stack.

$AVGO is one of the strongest large-cap photonics plays.

Broadcom already dominates: 🔌 networking 🔌 optical connectivity 🔌 silicon-photonics infrastructure 🔌 AI data-center interconnects

It sits directly at the AI bandwidth bottleneck.

$COHR is another critical player.

Coherent controls: 💡 industrial lasers 💡 optical subsystems 💡 photonic components 💡 semiconductor manufacturing optics

As photonic infrastructure scales, laser demand scales with it.

$LITE remains deeply leveraged to: 📡 fiber optics 📡 telecom photonics 📡 optical sensing 📡 AI networking expansion

The company effectively monetizes bandwidth growth itself.

$INTC is quietly positioning aggressively in silicon photonics.

Intel understands a critical reality:

Traditional scaling alone is no longer enough.

Future compute increasingly depends on: 💡 optical movement of data rather than purely electrical scaling.

Smaller pure-play names like: ⚡ $LASE ⚡ Ayar Labs (private) ⚡ Lightmatter (private)

represent the more speculative frontier.

Higher risk. Potentially enormous upside.

Especially if optical interconnect adoption accelerates faster than expected.

Emphasis

In photonics the bottlenecks are: ⚠️ optical integration ⚠️ laser systems ⚠️ advanced packaging ⚠️ thermal efficiency ⚠️ networking infrastructure

The defense angle matters enormously too.

Photonics is not just about AI chatbots.

It increasingly affects: 🛰 satellite systems 🎯 targeting infrastructure 📡 radar systems ⚔ electronic warfare 🤖 autonomous drones

This is becoming strategic infrastructure.

Another major theme:

Photonics helps reduce energy intensity.

This becomes critical because hyperscale AI now collides directly with: ⚡ grid shortages 💧 water scarcity 🔥 cooling constraints 🛢 diesel stress

The physical economy increasingly constrains digital expansion.

We have repeatedly emphasized:

The future AI race may not simply be about: “Who has the best model?”

It may become: “Who can physically power and cool computation?”

Photonics helps solve that problem.

This is why the market increasingly rewards: 🏗 infrastructure ⚡ utilities 💧 cooling 🧲 critical minerals 💡 photonic efficiency

The AI story is becoming deeply industrial.

The broader implication is profound:

Photonics may ultimately do to traditional electronics… …what the internet did to analog communication.

The infrastructure layer itself changes.

But the transition also accelerates concentration.

Only a narrowing circle of: 🏢 hyperscalers 🏦 mega-cap firms 🏭 industrial suppliers ⚔ defense-linked infrastructure players

…may fully benefit.

The K-shape deepens.

The irony is striking:

The AI revolution was originally marketed as democratizing technology.

Instead it increasingly centralizes: ⚡ compute ⚡ infrastructure ⚡ capital ⚡ energy access ⚡ photonic capability

into fewer hands.

We believe the market still underestimates how quickly photonics may become unavoidable.

Because the problem is no longer theoretical.

The electrical system itself is hitting physical limits.

The final takeaway:

The next generation of AI winners may not simply be software companies.

They may be the firms controlling: 💡 light transmission ⚡ power efficiency 🔥 thermal management 🏗 optical infrastructure

The future of computing increasingly belongs to photonics.

Modern AI infrastructure now faces: ⚠️ bandwidth ceilings ⚠️ power-consumption explosions ⚠️ heat-density problems ⚠️ latency bottlenecks ⚠️ interconnect saturation The current architecture is becoming unsustainable. This is where photonics enters. Photonics = using LIGHT (photons) instead of electricity (electrons) to: 💡 transmit data 💡 process signals 💡 move information 💡 scale compute infrastructure The implications are enormous. We believe photonics is no longer speculative science fiction. It is increasingly becoming: ⚠️ necessary ⚠️ commercially viable ⚠️ strategically essential Especially for hyperscale AI systems. The AI boom itself is creating the problem photonics solves. As models scale: 📈 compute demand explodes 📈 rack density rises 📈 cooling loads surge 📈 electricity consumption skyrockets Copper interconnects increasingly become the bottleneck. Traditional electrical systems waste massive amounts of energy as heat. Photonics changes the equation: ⚡ lower power consumption ⚡ faster transmission ⚡ lower latency ⚡ higher bandwidth ⚡ less thermal stress This is why hyperscalers are aggressively pursuing it. In this case the sequence is straightforward: Step 1: 🤖 AI models scale exponentially Step 2: ⚠️ Electrical interconnects hit limits Step 3: 🔥 Cooling and power costs explode Step 4: 💡 Photonics becomes economically necessary Step 5: 🏗 Entire data-center architecture changes This transition is already happening quietly. Over the next 3–5 years: ⚠️ co-packaged optics ⚠️ optical switching ⚠️ silicon photonics ⚠️ photonic networking …will increasingly replace traditional copper infrastructure. The implications go far beyond performance. Photonics may fundamentally reshape: 🏭 semiconductor design ⚡ power consumption 💧 cooling infrastructure 🧠 AI deployment economics 🏢 hyperscale construction We believe this becomes one of the clearest examples of K-shaped technological consolidation. Why? Because only the largest players can afford: 💰 photonic R&D 💰 custom AI infrastructure 💰 advanced packaging 💰 optical integration (Watch chip makers rotating, new partnerships) Smaller competitors increasingly get locked out. The photonics transition favors: ✅ hyperscalers ✅ mega-cap semiconductor firms ✅ defense-linked infrastructure players ✅ vertically integrated ecosystems The technological moat widens. 🚨 INVESTABLE PLAYERS Several companies already sit directly inside this transition. ⚡ Broadcom ($AVGO) ⚡ Coherent ($COHR) ⚡ Lumentum ($LITE) ⚡ Intel ($INTC) ⚡ Laser Photonics ($LASE) Each attacks a different layer of the stack. $AVGO is one of the strongest large-cap photonics plays. Broadcom already dominates: 🔌 networking 🔌 optical connectivity 🔌 silicon-photonics infrastructure 🔌 AI data-center interconnects It sits directly at the AI bandwidth bottleneck. $COHR is another critical player. Coherent controls: 💡 industrial lasers 💡 optical subsystems 💡 photonic components 💡 semiconductor manufacturing optics As photonic infrastructure scales, laser demand scales with it. $LITE remains deeply leveraged to: 📡 fiber optics 📡 telecom photonics 📡 optical sensing 📡 AI networking expansion The company effectively monetizes bandwidth growth itself. $INTC is quietly positioning aggressively in silicon photonics. Intel understands a critical reality: Traditional scaling alone is no longer enough. Future compute increasingly depends on: 💡 optical movement of data rather than purely electrical scaling. Smaller pure-play names like: ⚡ $LASE ⚡ Ayar Labs (private) ⚡ Lightmatter (private) represent the more speculative frontier. Higher risk. Potentially enormous upside. Especially if optical interconnect adoption accelerates faster than expected. Emphasis In photonics the bottlenecks are: ⚠️ optical integration ⚠️ laser systems ⚠️ advanced packaging ⚠️ thermal efficiency ⚠️ networking infrastructure The defense angle matters enormously too. Photonics is not just about AI chatbots. It increasingly affects: 🛰 satellite systems 🎯 targeting infrastructure 📡 radar systems ⚔ electronic warfare 🤖 autonomous drones This is becoming strategic infrastructure. Another major theme: Photonics helps reduce energy intensity. This becomes critical because hyperscale AI now collides directly with: ⚡ grid shortages 💧 water scarcity 🔥 cooling constraints 🛢 diesel stress The physical economy increasingly constrains digital expansion. We have repeatedly emphasized: The future AI race may not simply be about: “Who has the best model?” It may become: “Who can physically power and cool computation?” Photonics helps solve that problem. This is why the market increasingly rewards: 🏗 infrastructure ⚡ utilities 💧 cooling 🧲 critical minerals 💡 photonic efficiency The AI story is becoming deeply industrial. The broader implication is profound: Photonics may ultimately do to traditional electronics… …what the internet did to analog communication. The infrastructure layer itself changes. But the transition also accelerates concentration. Only a narrowing circle of: 🏢 hyperscalers 🏦 mega-cap firms 🏭 industrial suppliers ⚔ defense-linked infrastructure players …may fully benefit. The K-shape deepens. The irony is striking: The AI revolution was originally marketed as democratizing technology. Instead it increasingly centralizes: ⚡ compute ⚡ infrastructure ⚡ capital ⚡ energy access ⚡ photonic capability into fewer hands. We believe the market still underestimates how quickly photonics may become unavoidable. Because the problem is no longer theoretical. The electrical system itself is hitting physical limits. The final takeaway: The next generation of AI winners may not simply be software companies. They may be the firms controlling: 💡 light transmission ⚡ power efficiency 🔥 thermal management 🏗 optical infrastructure The future of computing increasingly belongs to photonics. #Photonics #AI #Semiconductors #SiliconPhotonics #DataCenters #Broadcom #AVGO #Coherent #COHR #Lumentum #LITE #Intel #LASE #QuantumComputing #Optics #Hyperscalers #Infrastructure #ArtificialIntelligence #Computing #CriticalMinerals #EnergyCrisis #KShapedEconomy #Investing #StockMarket #TechStocks #PiggosTradingDesk @DonMiami3 @Whitelavender92 @Diablo_Blanco_3 @Marbella_Media"}}}, "rest_id": "2062948497977196803", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "161", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

============================================================
SOLIDIFICATION OF LIGHT AND PROGRAMMABLE MATTER
Connection Brief
============================================================

Solidification of Light:
- Refers to experiments making photons behave like condensed matter.
- Key breakthrough (March 2025): Italian researchers created the first
  "photonic supersolid" — light that has a rigid crystalline structure
  (solid-like) while flowing without friction (superfluid-like).
- Achieved using exciton-polaritons in semiconductor nanostructures.

Programmable Matter:
- Materials that can change physical properties (shape, density,
  optical traits, stiffness) in a controlled/programmable way.
- Includes metamaterials, shape-changing systems, and modular robotics.

Connections:
1. Light as a programmable medium: Supersolid light and polariton
   systems allow dynamic, reconfigurable quantum states controlled
   by lasers or external fields.

2. Programmable photonics overlap: Solid-light phenomena can serve
   as building blocks for adaptive optical chips, tunable lasers,
   optical neural networks, and quantum simulators.

3. Hybrid future tech: Combines exotic quantum behavior of "solid"
   light with the reconfigurability of smart materials, enabling
   potential applications in quantum computing, adaptive optics,
   and light-based programmable devices.

Current status: Mostly laboratory-scale quantum experiments, but
strong conceptual bridge toward future smart photonic materials.

References:
[1] Live Science / Nature (2025) - Photonic supersolid creation
[2] Wikipedia / ScienceAlert - Solid light and supersolids
[3] Wikipedia - Programmable matter
[4] Related works on polaritons and programmable photonics

text:
Researchers at Monash University have developed a tiny chip that could help pave the way for computers that process information using light instead of electricity. Published in the journal Nature Photonics, the study demonstrates a nanoscale device capable of generating, controlling and reading light-based signals on a single platform. The breakthrough uses an emerging technology called valleytronics, which relies on quantum properties in advanced materials to encode information. Researchers say the innovation could lead to faster computing, lower energy consumption and more efficient communication systems in the future.

Investigadores de la Universidad Monash desarrollaron un diminuto chip que podría ayudar a abrir el camino hacia computadoras capaces de procesar información utilizando luz en lugar de electricidad. Publicado en la revista Nature Photonics, el estudio demuestra un dispositivo a escala nanométrica capaz de generar, controlar y leer señales basadas en luz en una sola plataforma. El avance utiliza una tecnología emergente conocida como valleytrónica, que aprovecha propiedades cuánticas de materiales avanzados para codificar información. Los investigadores afirman que la innovación podría conducir a una computación más rápida, un menor consumo de energía y sistemas de comunicación más eficientes en el futuro.

text:
Karnataka CM @DKShivakumar on Friday met a high-level delegation from the German state of Thuringia, led by Minister-President Mario Voigt, to discuss strengthening economic ties and investment opportunities. 

Heavy Industries Minister @MBPatil said Karnataka and Thuringia can collaborate in optics, photonics, semiconductors, biotech, EV manufacturing, renewable energy and advanced manufacturing. Shivakumar highlighted opportunities in technology transfer, innovation, skill exchange and next-generation technologies. Voigt praised Karnataka’s R&D ecosystem, startup network, engineering talent, quantum technology & aerospace infrastructure.

Photonics bottlenecks go way beyond lasers. 

CPO/NPO integration, thermal mgmt, and OCS switches are key scalers. Diversify across the chain for AI cluster exposure. 

Pluggables probably start ramping in 2027, CPO follows. High TAM but watch yields, hyperscaler quals & execution risks. 

Picks & shovels win regardless.

text:
While Nasdaq is down 3%, the number one gainer on the S&P today is FedEx Freight $FDXF at +9%.

But, no one cared about this 5 days ago, because it's not related to bottlenecks in photonics.

Yet, trucking - especially LTL - is a bottleneck. Plus, the re-rating vs. $ODFL, $SAIA, and $XPO after spin.

Though, it's too boring for X, and such posts are becoming increasingly a waste of time.

This was #1 reason I've kept $FDX but sold my $UPS couple years ago. The theoretical value of the distributed stake implied an initial $FDXF price in the mid-to-high $170s range. As typical with spin-offs, they get sold and right now we're $150s. Estimated FY2026 revenue: $8.7B (largest North American LTL carrier by revenue). It carries major debt due to a $4.1B cash dividend paid to FedEx pre-spin. Old Dominion $ODFL: EV/EBITDA 25–27x, PE 43–47x. Superior operating ratio 72–75% range historically/recently and consistent execution. Saia $SAIA and $XPO: Also premium high 40+ PE, strong EV/EBITDA, with growth/margin stories. Potential $FDFX catalysts: - Immediate S&P 500 and Dow Jones Transportation Average inclusion (index buying). - Pure-play LTL focus vs. being buried inside $FDX . - Potential for margin/ROIC improvement under dedicated management and capex/IT focus. -Long-term re-rating opportunity if it narrows the efficiency gap toward $ODFL $SAIA levels. I added 50 shares simply because the 50 I got is too small. Similar to a semiconductor IPO, hard to think of a better time in recent history to do a trucking IPO/spinoff."}}}, "rest_id": "2061539530730254635", "source": "Twitter Web Client", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "16031", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2062943437528404282", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "11373", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
PhotonVentures is a European venture capital fund dedicated to integrated photonics, investing in companies from seed to Series A and supporting their transition from early validation to scalable businesses. As a specialized deep tech investor, the fund combines capital with direct access to a global ecosystem of foundries, designers, and system integrators via PhotonDelta.

This article outlines how investors evaluate early-stage photonic chip startups, what differentiates investment-ready teams, and how companies can position themselves for scale, drawing on the perspective of PhotonVentures. It focuses on the practical signals investors look for across technology, market readiness, and ecosystem alignment.

Full interview here: https://t.co/HKQNA1CSJP

#engineering #technology #photonics #PICs

@photondelta

4007}, {"from_index": 4787, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 4792}, {"from_index": 4831, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 4847}]}, "text": "Copper can't keep up with AI. That's not an opinion, it's physics. Every data center being built right now is replacing electrical connections with light. NVIDIA confirmed it with $4.5 billion in direct investment. I mapped 25 public companies across the photonics value chain: Every AI cluster being built today hits the same wall. A hundred thousand GPUs mean nothing if the data can't move between them fast enough. Copper maxed out years ago and photonics replaced it: lasers, optical fiber, and transceivers that push data at the speed of light. The AI transceiver market doubled in two years. NVIDIA committed $4.5 billion across three photonics companies this year alone. This is where the infrastructure money is going. Here's the full value chain: 🔬 MATERIALS & WAFERS This is the bottom of the chain. Every laser and transceiver starts as a wafer substrate: indium phosphide, gallium arsenide, germanium, specialty glass. Nobody above this layer can produce anything without these inputs, and right now the most critical one, indium phosphide, is the tightest material in the entire AI supply chain. The gap between demand and capacity is getting worse, not better. I think this is the most asymmetric layer on the map. Investors chase the transceiver companies and ignore who grows the substrates underneath them. But NVIDIA is writing checks worth billions in cash and warrants to lock up supply from this exact layer. First link in the chain, last to get attention, and the one that chokes everything above it if it breaks. Tickers: $GLW, $AXTI, $IQE, $AIXA, $AMS 💡 CORE PHOTONIC DEVICES This layer converts electricity into light and back. Without it, zero data moves through fiber. NVIDIA dropped $4 billion into two companies here this year just to secure production capacity, and both of them joined the S&P 500 within weeks of each other. That should tell you how fast this went from niche to essential. The supply gap is not closing. The companies shipping next gen lasers at volume can be counted on one hand, and switching suppliers takes years of requalification. Order books stretch past twelve months. Every next generation GPU cluster consumes more of these components than the last, and no one can substitute them on short notice. I watch this layer more closely than any other. Tickers: $IPGP, $COHR, $LITE, $LASR, $SIVE 🔌 COMPONENTS & MODULES The companies here take raw lasers and detectors, package them into finished transceivers and modules, and ship them straight to hyperscalers. If the layers below are the engine, this is the vehicle that actually reaches the customer. Hyperscaler purchase orders land here. The revenue acceleration shows up here first. What I like about this layer is that you can underwrite it today, not in two years. These are businesses with signed capacity commitments and product already moving. The consolidation angle matters too: larger photonics players have already started absorbing standalone module companies, and whoever remains independent gains pricing power as options thin out. Tickers: $AAOI, $MTSI, $VIAV, $LPTH ⚙️ SYSTEMS & EQUIPMENT No company above this layer can manufacture a single photonic component without the machines built here. One of these names holds 100% of the EUV lithography market with zero competitors. Others supply the bonding equipment for co packaged optics or the process control instruments used across the majority of advanced packaging lines. If photonics is the gold rush, this is the layer selling the picks. My honest take: this is where the smart, patient capital parks. Equipment companies have pricing power and multi year order books that generate cash through full capex cycles. They attract holders who don't panic on the first pullback. The stocks don't run 1,000% overnight, but they compound while everything above them swings, and that tradeoff is worth more than most people give it credit for. Tickers: $ASML, $BESI, $ASM, $LPKF, $MKSI 🔍 TEST, METROLOGY & YIELD The most ignored layer on this map, and arguably the one with the cleanest business model. Every wafer, laser, and transceiver has to be tested and verified before it ships. As speeds climb and photonic devices get more complex, the testing challenge compounds fast. The industry is now constrained not only by what it can build but by what it can prove actually works. Yield is money. Better defect detection means better margins for every company upstream, which is why foundries keep buying test equipment even when they slash budgets everywhere else. These are capital light businesses tied to every unit of production across the chain. Last check before product hits the customer, and one of the few layers where demand doesn't cycle down when the rest of semis softens. Tickers: $CAMT, $FORM, $AEHR, $ONTO, $VIAV 🧠FINAL THOUGHTS The NVIDIA capital concentration tells the whole story. One company wrote $4.5 billion in checks to three photonics suppliers in a single quarter. That is a company locking down the one input that could bottleneck its GPU deployments: the optical interconnect. Returns across this sector have been historic over the past twelve months. But separate the revenue growers from the narrative trades. Some of these companies are printing real quarterly numbers that would impress in any sector. Others are carrying multi billion dollar market caps on sub $100 million in annual revenue. Same sector, wildly different risk. Every generation of AI infrastructure from here forward needs more photonics. Not less. The copper to light transition inside data centers is early. Co packaged optics is barely in deployment, and 1.6T transceivers are ramping with 3.2T already on roadmaps. The chain locks together: stress on any single link reprices every link above it."}}}, "rest_id": "2062502220889837836", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "267657", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2062936149908213920", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "49", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
An investor with over 7 years of experience as a $SIVE shareholder

Maintains a clearly optimistic view on the future of the company

Highlights its strategic positioning in cutting edge technologies such as:

- Integrated photonics

- Optical solutions for AI data centers

- High-performance wireless communications

- Defense applications

The investor considers that recent criticism appearing in Swedish media

Short seller reports

And share sales by former executives represent temporary "noise" rather than anything else

Which does not reflect the company's fundamental reality

In their opinion, these negative elements overshadow significant achievements

Like important strategic alliances:

- Partnership with $GFS

- Ayar Labs entering the $NVDA ecosystem

- Inclusion in the MSCI Small Caps

- Nasdaq dual listing

And a solid pipeline of opportunities hovering around $800M

With 77% growth in a quarter

With the global AI boom as a backdrop

And backed by external validations such as CHIPS Act funding

The $SIVE shareholder invites interested parties to do their own research

Reminding that strong convictions often feel lonely before becoming obvious to the market

I couldn't agree more with their opinion

Bullish as ever on $SIVE

a Seven-Year Shareholder: Why Sivers Semiconductors / $SIVE Was Always Destined for Greatness After nearly seven years as a shareholder in Sivers Semiconductors, I have watched this company evolve from an overlooked Swedish deeptech player into a force at the intersection of photonics, wireless communications, AI infrastructure, and strategic defense technologies. The recent surge in the share price is not a surprise to those of us who have followed the story closely it is the logical unfolding of a thesis many of us articulated years ago when few were listening. From the moment I first dug into the company, its potential felt enormous and profoundly underappreciated. The technology advanced lasers for co-packaged optics in AI data centers, beamforming ICs for 5G/6G and SATCOM, and full-duplex arrays for electronic warfare sits at the heart of multiple secular megatrends. Yet for years, the market fixated on quarterly losses, development costs, and execution risks while largely ignoring the customer pipeline, partnerships, and technological edge. Where Unicorns Are Born This is how real winners emerge. History is clear: transformative companies are rarely discovered in consensus comfort zones. They are unearthed where sentiment is exhausted, where weak hands have capitulated, and where the narrative is dominated by skepticism. Sivers spent years in that crucible not for the faint-hearted. You had to immerse yourself in the details: the photonics platform’s unique indium phosphide capabilities and its work with hyperscalers, AI data center players, $AAPL (sensing) and Win Semiconductors, along with key partners such as Jabil and GlobalFoundries; the wireless division’s tier-1 engagements with names like Nokia, BAE Systems, SATCOM operators and the U.S. government itself. Companies and institutions of that caliber do not collaborate, co-develop, or plan multi-year programs with you unless there is real substance and a clear path forward. That, for me, is the only validation you truly need. Add to that the expanding opportunity pipeline now approaching $800 million and strategic validations like repeat US CHIPS Act funding, and the picture becomes very clear. Bears and headline-chasers thrive on the opposite approach. Swedish media have produced more than 50 negative articles in recent times the positive ones you can count on one hand. It is a witch hunt. They obsessively highlight risk, risk, risk, with almost no mention of the enormous potential. How can anyone take them seriously? They have no skin in the game. If they could invest successfully, they wouldn’t be working for newspapers. They pretend to be all-knowing, but the truth is no one knows exactly where technology and the market are heading. After two decades working with IT companies large and small I understand both the tech and the market. That is why I am bullish, and why this development does not surprise me. The same goes for the short sellers. One recent 43-page “analysis” packed with incorrect assumptions and misinformation was clearly designed to create fear and doubt. I could refute every single point, but why give them more oxygen? Notably, they don’t even dare to hold a meaningful disclosed short position. That is weaker than their analysis. I put real money on the table and take real risk. Please increase your shorts. I am waiting for you to go under. Leadership and Shareholder Transitions: A Healthy Reset Some former insiders and larger holders have exited. I am not surprised I am relieved. They did not deserve to be part of the journey that is now unfolding, a journey that is still far from over. Erik Fällström and his associates supported the company for many years, and for that we are grateful. But the attempts to extract personal gain crossed a line most notably trying to spin off the photonics division into a SPAC where he (via Achilles Capital / DDM) was a major sponsor, at what looked like bargain terms. The chairman and new CEO rightly put a stop to it. Shortly after, the selling began. He sold the majority of his holdings around 4 SEK. Karma is real. In parallel, Achilles Capital and its parent DDM Finance have been forced to apply for corporate reconstruction due to massive debt issues. The contrast between opportunism and long-term conviction could not be clearer. Harish Krishnaswamy came in via the MixComm acquisition. I will not lie I like Harish. He is technically strong and has an excellent network that helped land key development agreements, including CHIPS Act wins. At the same time, I am not surprised he is selling. He has sold multiple times before, often at 4–8 SEK levels. This latest sale seems to be his chance to redeem all the earlier exits at much lower prices a kind of psychological average to finally make it feel better. Am I happy about it? No. But this is the same person who, several times right after Sivers secured major agreements, sold and killed the momentum, or sold when tax bills came due. As one of the founders, I think he simply wanted to feel that his years at MixComm finally delivered something tangible. This was the last time, and frankly, it feels damn good. Funds that fully exited did so because they operate under strict risk mandates and manage other people’s money. That is natural and not a negative signal. These departures represent a healthy cleansing. The right people and the right long-term capital are now aligned for the next phase. The Path Forward No one has a crystal ball. No one knows exactly how large the Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) market will ultimately become. But I genuinely believe people will be shocked by the speed and momentum once it really starts ramping. The combination of exploding AI compute demand, power constraints in data centers, and Sivers’ differentiated indium phosphide laser platform positions the company at the center of one of the most important technology shifts of this decade. The current momentum validates what patient shareholders have long seen. Product ramps, pipeline conversion, CHIPS Act milestones, and potential US dual-listing preparations are tangible progress. Volatility will remain; bears will resurface. But conviction built on deep research outlasts noise. To newer investors: Do your own work. Ignore the echo chamber of fear. Build your own mosaic from primary sources. True edges come from independent thinking. The easy path is skepticism and short-termism. The harder, more rewarding one is sustained belief grounded in analysis. Sivers was never a quick flip. It was and remains a multi-year compounder for those willing to look beyond the noise. The diamond was always there for those with the eyes to see it. The journey is far from over, and the best chapters are still ahead. Stay bullish. Stay informed. And above all, trust the work you’ve done."}}}, "rest_id": "2063251944550838728", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "240281", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2063366985308852538", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "8320", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

TSMC’s COUPE™ platform is one to watch.
It brings #siliconphotonics into the advanced packaging stack: combining CoWoS®, SoIC®, advanced logic, HBM, interposers, embedded components, and optical engine in one HPC platform for AI.
This is the technology foundation underpinning  NVIDIA’s CPO roadmap: moving optical I/O closer to the compute fabric to unlock higher bandwidth, lower latency, and better power efficiency for next-gen AI clusters.
Silicon photonics is not just a networking add-on. It is becoming 
is becoming part of the AI packaging stack.

text:
$SIVE delivers another very interesting move today, +13.45% (close 89.40 SEK), approaching the upper resistance zone marked at 85–90 SEK — after an aggressive rejection off the support zone at 74.50–78.50 SEK. 

The name continues to trade very extended, with a YTD return exceeding +2,000%, but that didn't stop me from taking exposure sub-40 SEK and DCA-ing at 60–65 SEK. 

The company has serious potential — recently discovered and now being accumulated by institutionals: JPMorgan disclosed a 5.25% stake in the company, its first-ever position in the stock. 

Having institutionals on our side confirms a lot. Imagine this name eventually listing on Nasdaq — access opens dramatically wider for investment funds and everyone who has missed the move so far. 

There are plenty of bearish theories that say it'll round-trip back to where it came from, but I'm very satisfied with the decision so far and will hold it for a longer period until I'm satisfied with the gain — currently +160%+, with higher targets in mind. 

The photonics names have momentum again — they keep getting bought after every dip, which suggests the move can continue.

text:
"North America sales halved purely from US defense budget approvals slipping into H2"

--> Sivers' photonics division doesn't have material U.S. defense photonics revenue to lose. Their photonics business is a LiDAR/CPO play - automotive and datacom, not defense radar or military satcom. The CHIPS Act award applied to their wireless/RF division, not photonics. Photonics revenue was DOWN 32% from last year. 

--> The opportunity pipeline is meaningless word salad. 

--> Speaking of dilution, we happen to know exactly when and where the next round is! On June 15, at their Annual General Meeting, the board will pass a 15% dilution round. 

If you absolutely must buy into this amazing "growth" story, probably wait until that passes. 

Good luck!

Opened a small position in $META. Looking into OTM leaps. 

$AMZN Bought OTM leap. Hoping for some consolidation around $240 / 245'ish. 

$CRDO decent price action on this name thus far. 
$AAOI so far I've only traded it ST twice 170+ to $200+ rinse & repeat. Options look bullish, 

 I like the sector going into end of year so any name in CPO's photonics would be a good choice. I'll personally be a bit more patient while building out positions as the MAG7 have shown some weakness lately and retesting some levels that would give us some indication about the next few months.

text:
$ASYS vs $VECO 

Which one's better?

Here's a review:

$ASYS just printed 31% y/y revenue growth in its latest quarter, expanded gross margins to 47.7% on the shift to higher-ASP AI packaging products, and moved solidly back into profitability. It trades at roughly 4x sales.

$VECO trades at a similar 3.7-4x sales. It is larger ($2.4B market cap vs $320M), more established, and carries exposure to the hot silicon photonics wave for AI optical interconnects. Both operate in the advanced packaging and process equipment layer that enables AI GPUs, chiplets, and photonics.

So why nearly identical multiples when one is smaller, growing faster in the core AI segment right now, and printing much higher gross margins?

Let's walk through it.

$ASYS specializes in thermal processing solutions: precision reflow ovens, belt furnaces, and related tools that handle the critical solder reflow and thermal steps in advanced semiconductor packaging for AI GPUs, chiplets, and high-power devices. This is the specialized “assembly oven” layer that enables high-yield, high-throughput packaging of complex multi-die architectures.

$VECO offers a broader set of process tools: wet processing/clean/etch platforms (recently qualified for new advanced packaging applications), laser spike annealing, ion beam deposition, plus MOCVD and tools supporting silicon photonics fabrication. Complementary steps in the packaging and photonics manufacturing flow.

They are not direct one-for-one competitors on every product, but they sit adjacent in the advanced packaging equipment supply chain for AI. Leading OSATs and IDMs building next-gen AI infrastructure often use tools from both (plus others) to complete the packaging and optical engine builds. You need robust thermal/reflow capability as part of the flows that VECO’s wet/anneal tools support in other steps.

In fact, strong AI packaging demand is pulling on the entire equipment ecosystem.

Here is why the market prices them so closely:

$VECO is the bigger, more diversified platform with scale across semiconductor, silicon photonics (AI optical I/O), data storage recovery, and scientific markets. More institutional coverage, better liquidity, and a “portfolio” story that captures multiple AI infrastructure vectors.

$ASYS is the smaller, more concentrated pure-play on the thermal/reflow step specifically for AI advanced packaging. Cleaner narrative, less exposed to cyclical data storage, but it carries the natural microcap liquidity and coverage discount.

$ASYS has already executed the mix shift. AI-related revenue is now >30% of its Thermal Processing segment and expected to exceed 40% soon, driving gross margins from deeply negative territory last year to 47%+ today with high incremental margins as volume scales.

$ASYS runs an asset-light model with focused subsidiaries and recently raised capital to accelerate growth and M&A. Order momentum and backlog are building on AI packaging demand.

That explains a lot of the similarity in multiples. But here is what $ASYS brings that creates the opportunity:

- Superior margin profile and operating leverage right now. The AI packaging reflow products carry high ASPs and high gross margins. As that mix climbs further, margins expand with limited opex growth.

- More direct, leveraged exposure to the AI GPU/chiplet packaging capex wave. This is one of the most capacity-constrained and critical parts of the AI buildout. Advanced thermal processes are essential for yield and reliability in high-density, high-power packages.

- Smaller base + re-rating torque. Proving sustained growth and 45%+ gross margins in a high-demand niche can support a move to 6-8x sales (still reasonable for this profile). That would be substantial upside from current levels. VECO already prices in more of its scale and photonics breadth.

- Clean U.S.-based positioning serving leading global packaging houses ramping AI products, with no China manufacturing overhang that affects some other names in the broader chain.

The takeaway and my thoughts:

I am not saying $ASYS deserves a massive premium to $VECO (but I do think this is the case lol). Scale, diversification (including photonics), and platform breadth at VECO justify a similar multiple.

But two names in the AI advanced packaging/photonics equipment ecosystem, both around 4x sales, where the smaller one is growing faster in its core AI segment, has dramatically higher and still-expanding gross margins, a cleaner focused story, and more asymmetric upside from here… that is worth sitting with.

$ASYS also has a more concentrated bet on the exact packaging equipment step seeing intense pull from every major AI accelerator designer moving to chiplet architectures.

What do you think?

Do you think $ASYS offers better risk/reward than $VECO (the larger peer in the same niche)?

I certainly do.

$ASYS is also breaking out of a 30 year support/resistance channel looking to breakout and use this as support.

$VECO already had its breakout (still seems like it wants to go up, just not as much as $ASYS here).

That's why I am long $ASYS.

If you guys got any other tickers that you want me to compare to each other, send them down below.

Here's a review: $ASYS just printed 31% y/y revenue growth in its latest quarter, expanded gross margins to 47.7% on the shift to higher-ASP AI packaging products, and moved solidly back into profitability. It trades at roughly 4x sales. $VECO trades at a similar 3.7-4x sales. It is larger ($2.4B market cap vs $320M), more established, and carries exposure to the hot silicon photonics wave for AI optical interconnects. Both operate in the advanced packaging and process equipment layer that enables AI GPUs, chiplets, and photonics. So why nearly identical multiples when one is smaller, growing faster in the core AI segment right now, and printing much higher gross margins? Let's walk through it. $ASYS specializes in thermal processing solutions: precision reflow ovens, belt furnaces, and related tools that handle the critical solder reflow and thermal steps in advanced semiconductor packaging for AI GPUs, chiplets, and high-power devices. This is the specialized “assembly oven” layer that enables high-yield, high-throughput packaging of complex multi-die architectures. $VECO offers a broader set of process tools: wet processing/clean/etch platforms (recently qualified for new advanced packaging applications), laser spike annealing, ion beam deposition, plus MOCVD and tools supporting silicon photonics fabrication. Complementary steps in the packaging and photonics manufacturing flow. They are not direct one-for-one competitors on every product, but they sit adjacent in the advanced packaging equipment supply chain for AI. Leading OSATs and IDMs building next-gen AI infrastructure often use tools from both (plus others) to complete the packaging and optical engine builds. You need robust thermal/reflow capability as part of the flows that VECO’s wet/anneal tools support in other steps. In fact, strong AI packaging demand is pulling on the entire equipment ecosystem. Here is why the market prices them so closely: $VECO is the bigger, more diversified platform with scale across semiconductor, silicon photonics (AI optical I/O), data storage recovery, and scientific markets. More institutional coverage, better liquidity, and a “portfolio” story that captures multiple AI infrastructure vectors. $ASYS is the smaller, more concentrated pure-play on the thermal/reflow step specifically for AI advanced packaging. Cleaner narrative, less exposed to cyclical data storage, but it carries the natural microcap liquidity and coverage discount. $ASYS has already executed the mix shift. AI-related revenue is now >30% of its Thermal Processing segment and expected to exceed 40% soon, driving gross margins from deeply negative territory last year to 47%+ today with high incremental margins as volume scales. $ASYS runs an asset-light model with focused subsidiaries and recently raised capital to accelerate growth and M&A. Order momentum and backlog are building on AI packaging demand. That explains a lot of the similarity in multiples. But here is what $ASYS brings that creates the opportunity: - Superior margin profile and operating leverage right now. The AI packaging reflow products carry high ASPs and high gross margins. As that mix climbs further, margins expand with limited opex growth. - More direct, leveraged exposure to the AI GPU/chiplet packaging capex wave. This is one of the most capacity-constrained and critical parts of the AI buildout. Advanced thermal processes are essential for yield and reliability in high-density, high-power packages. - Smaller base + re-rating torque. Proving sustained growth and 45%+ gross margins in a high-demand niche can support a move to 6-8x sales (still reasonable for this profile). That would be substantial upside from current levels. VECO already prices in more of its scale and photonics breadth. - Clean U.S.-based positioning serving leading global packaging houses ramping AI products, with no China manufacturing overhang that affects some other names in the broader chain. The takeaway and my thoughts: I am not saying $ASYS deserves a massive premium to $VECO (but I do think this is the case lol). Scale, diversification (including photonics), and platform breadth at VECO justify a similar multiple. But two names in the AI advanced packaging/photonics equipment ecosystem, both around 4x sales, where the smaller one is growing faster in its core AI segment, has dramatically higher and still-expanding gross margins, a cleaner focused story, and more asymmetric upside from here… that is worth sitting with. $ASYS also has a more concentrated bet on the exact packaging equipment step seeing intense pull from every major AI accelerator designer moving to chiplet architectures. What do you think? Do you think $ASYS offers better risk/reward than $VECO (the larger peer in the same niche)? I certainly do. $ASYS is also breaking out of a 30 year support/resistance channel looking to breakout and use this as support. $VECO already had its breakout (still seems like it wants to go up, just not as much as $ASYS here). That's why I am long $ASYS. If you guys got any other tickers that you want me to compare to each other, send them down below."}}}, "rest_id": "2064067131327877327", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "3285", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

This is the backbone of AI scaling

text:
$AAOI - Zoom out to the prize

How fast is the CPO market $AAOI feeds actually growing?

Per Yole Group, the most respected analyst in photonics, pure co-packaged optics scales from ~$46M (2024) to ~$8.1B (2030). 

That’s a 137% CAGR - one of the steepest curves anywhere in AI infrastructure (~176× in six years).

A vertically integrated laser/light-source supplier sitting dead center of that ramp is exactly the kind of name you want to own early. 

(Source: Yole Group — “Co-Packaged Optics for Data Centers 2025.”)

text:
MY PORTFOLIO EXPLAINED
I have been meticulously building a portfolio based on the thesis that the Physical AI revolution is upon us.
I have chosen each of these companies, both for what they do and for providing asymmetrical return opportunity to investors. 

$SIVE  Sivers Semiconductors 
Sivers makes high-frequency radio frequency (RF) millimeter-wave (mmWave) transceivers and advanced optical semiconductors through its Wireless and Photonics divisions. In the AI ecosystem, the company is a critical enabler of next-generation data centers, collaborating with foundry giants like GlobalFoundries to integrate its laser arrays into silicon photonics and co-packaged optics (CPO) platforms to eliminate interconnect bandwidth bottlenecks for massive workloads. Concurrently, its mmWave technologies supply high-speed wireless connectivity for satellite communications, 5G telecom, and edge infrastructure, providing the high-throughput network backbone necessary for real-time edge AI coordination and smart cities.  

$LPK LPKF Laser & Electronics 
LPKF makes high-precision, laser-based micro-material manufacturing systems, headlined by its proprietary Laser Induced Deep Etching (LIDE) technology. As conventional organic substrates struggle with heavy thermal loads and warpage from massive chiplet integration, LPKF provides the precise tools needed to drill defect-free through-glass vias (TGVs) at aspect ratios up to 1:50 with zero micro-cracks. This specialized capability directly underpins advanced hardware and high-performance computing, enabling leading chipmakers to scale up substrate sizes, integrate high-bandwidth memory (HBM), and implement in-package microfluidic cooling for the next wave of physical AI chips.  

$ACCON Acconeer
Acconeer makes ultra-low-power, miniature 60 GHz Pulsed Coherent Radar (PCR) sensors that fuse millimeter-level depth precision with an exceptionally small spatial footprint. Within Edge AI and robotics, Acconeer’s chips allow tiny, battery-constrained hardware to execute real-time motion tracking, presence detection, and material or surface classification without relying on heavy cloud computation or invasive optical cameras. By deploying deep learning interfaces that compile models directly onto its integrated microcontrollers, the company provides robots, drones, and consumer devices with the localized spatial awareness required for autonomous navigation, gesture control, and power-efficient smart presence sensing.  

$SILC  Silicom 
Silicom designs and manufactures high-performance networking and data infrastructure hardware, including server network interface cards (NICs), programmable FPGA-based acceleration cards, SmartNICs, and edge switches. The company anchors Edge AI and smart systems by offloading heavy encryption, data compression, and packet-routing tasks from host CPUs, allowing decentralized AI infrastructure to maintain ultra-low latency and maximum data throughput. Operating primarily via design wins with cybersecurity firms, hyperscalers, and telcos, Silicom enables the secure, low-latency processing fabrics required for distributed AI inference networks and 5G/6G open radio access network (O-RAN) rollouts.  

$GAPW-B Gapwaves
Gapwaves develops and fabricates specialized, millimeter-wave antennas utilizing its patented gap-waveguide technology. This low-loss, cost-efficient hardware is engineered to maximize the resolution, beam direction, and overall range of high-frequency radar systems without increasing power consumption. Gapwaves fits squarely into the autonomous systems and smart city landscapes by supplying Tier-1 automotive partners with the structural building blocks for next-generation ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems), enabling autonomous vehicles, industrial transport robots, and urban traffic monitors to build reliable, high-definition models of their surroundings under any weather condition.

$SEYE Smart Eye
Smart Eye makes computer-vision hardware and software platforms dedicated to Human Insight AI, tracking eye gaze, facial expressions, and head movements in real time. The company’s primary footprint is in the automotive sector, where its edge-based Driver Monitoring Systems (DMS) and interior cabin sensing modules identify operator distraction, drowsiness, and cognitive load to comply with global safety mandates. This technology bridges the human-machine interface across smart transportation and autonomous vehicles, while its subsidiaries (like Affectiva) extend this technology into emotion AI, which is increasingly utilized in behavioral research, aerospace simulators, and interactive robotics.

$AMBA Ambarella
Ambarella builds ultra-low-power, high-efficiency system-on-chip (SoC) vision and radar processors built on its algorithm-first CVflow artificial intelligence architecture. Its chips, like the 4nm CV7 and the high-compute N1 series, serve as the localized brain for Edge AI, industrial robotics, and smart city infrastructure by running complex convolutional and transformer neural networks simultaneously. By integrating advanced image signal processing, hardware-accelerated video encoding, and native neural network computation on a single die, Ambarella allows security cameras, aerial drones, and autonomous mobile robots (AMRs) to execute edge-based multi-sensor perception, visual odometry, and real-time SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) entirely disconnected from the cloud.

$OUST Ouster
 Ouster designs and manufactures high-resolution 3D digital LiDAR sensors alongside integrated perception suites, following its strategic combination with Stereolabs' ZED camera technology. By embedding native color and precise spatial mapping directly onto its proprietary silicon architecture, Ouster creates an end-to-end sensing platform for Physical AI. This hardware acts as the primary sensory organ for humanoid robotics, warehouse automation, autonomous mining fleets, and smart city infrastructure (via its BlueCity traffic management software), delivering low-latency, resilient environmental data that allows machines to map, track, and safely navigate complex physical spaces.

$AMZN Amazon
Within the AI and robotics verticals, Amazon develops custom infrastructure silicon—such as its proprietary Trainium and Inferentia acceleration chips—while engineering vast, automated logistics systems for its global fulfillment network. The company is a pioneer in warehouse robotics, deploying fleets of fully autonomous mobile robots like Proteus and intelligent manipulation systems like Sparrow to streamline its supply chain via machine learning and reinforcement learning. On the edge, Amazon implements localized AI and natural language processing through consumer ecosystems like Alexa, Ring, and the Astro home robot, establishing a ubiquitous presence across cloud AI architecture, industrial automation, and smart home systems.

$ELMT Elmet Group
Elmet Group fabricates precision-engineered components and high-power radio frequency (RF) or microwave equipment, operating through its Critical Materials Components and Engineered Microwave Products divisions. Specializing in the custom manufacturing of refractory metals like pure tungsten and molybdenum, Elmet supplies the foundational, high-durability hardware components required for advanced semiconductor fabrication, aerospace systems, and defense electronics. It fits into the advanced hardware and AI supply chain by delivering the critical thermal management substrates, ion implantation shielding, and robust RF distribution modules necessary to manufacture and run high-energy compute chips.

$TRT Trio-Tech International 
Trio-Tech provides semiconductor back-end testing services and manufactures heavy-duty reliability equipment, including customized burn-in boards, wet process stations, and environmental testing chambers. The company serves as a vital gatekeeper for advanced hardware reliability, validating that complex chips can withstand severe thermal and electrical stress without failing prior to commercial rollout. Driven by the massive ramp-up in next-generation computing architectures, Trio-Tech directly supports AI and smart automotive expansion by stress-testing high-performance GPUs, CPUs, and electric vehicle (EV) electronics across its key facilities in Southeast Asia.

$HLIT Harmonic
Harmonic produces virtualized broadband access networks and software-based cloud video delivery infrastructure, known for solutions like its VOS360 cloud platform and cOS applications. The company embeds AI into smart systems by leveraging Content-Aware Encoding (CAE) algorithms and dynamic ad-insertion technologies to process mass media streams at the network edge, squeezing bandwidth requirements by up to 50% without compromising visual quality. This positions Harmonic as a fundamental utility provider for edge systems and smart cities, optimizing the heavy data pipelines that distribute live television, ultra-high-definition streaming, and localized broadband connectivity at scale.

{"from_index": 8357, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 8365}]}, "text": "MY PORTFOLIO EXPLAINED I have been meticulously building a portfolio based on the thesis that the Physical AI revolution is upon us. I have chosen each of these companies, both for what they do and for providing asymmetrical return opportunity to investors. $SIVE Sivers Semiconductors Sivers makes high-frequency radio frequency (RF) millimeter-wave (mmWave) transceivers and advanced optical semiconductors through its Wireless and Photonics divisions. In the AI ecosystem, the company is a critical enabler of next-generation data centers, collaborating with foundry giants like GlobalFoundries to integrate its laser arrays into silicon photonics and co-packaged optics (CPO) platforms to eliminate interconnect bandwidth bottlenecks for massive workloads. Concurrently, its mmWave technologies supply high-speed wireless connectivity for satellite communications, 5G telecom, and edge infrastructure, providing the high-throughput network backbone necessary for real-time edge AI coordination and smart cities.   $LPK LPKF Laser & Electronics LPKF makes high-precision, laser-based micro-material manufacturing systems, headlined by its proprietary Laser Induced Deep Etching (LIDE) technology. As conventional organic substrates struggle with heavy thermal loads and warpage from massive chiplet integration, LPKF provides the precise tools needed to drill defect-free through-glass vias (TGVs) at aspect ratios up to 1:50 with zero micro-cracks. This specialized capability directly underpins advanced hardware and high-performance computing, enabling leading chipmakers to scale up substrate sizes, integrate high-bandwidth memory (HBM), and implement in-package microfluidic cooling for the next wave of physical AI chips.   $ACCON Acconeer Acconeer makes ultra-low-power, miniature 60 GHz Pulsed Coherent Radar (PCR) sensors that fuse millimeter-level depth precision with an exceptionally small spatial footprint. Within Edge AI and robotics, Acconeer’s chips allow tiny, battery-constrained hardware to execute real-time motion tracking, presence detection, and material or surface classification without relying on heavy cloud computation or invasive optical cameras. By deploying deep learning interfaces that compile models directly onto its integrated microcontrollers, the company provides robots, drones, and consumer devices with the localized spatial awareness required for autonomous navigation, gesture control, and power-efficient smart presence sensing.   $SILC Silicom Silicom designs and manufactures high-performance networking and data infrastructure hardware, including server network interface cards (NICs), programmable FPGA-based acceleration cards, SmartNICs, and edge switches. The company anchors Edge AI and smart systems by offloading heavy encryption, data compression, and packet-routing tasks from host CPUs, allowing decentralized AI infrastructure to maintain ultra-low latency and maximum data throughput. Operating primarily via design wins with cybersecurity firms, hyperscalers, and telcos, Silicom enables the secure, low-latency processing fabrics required for distributed AI inference networks and 5G/6G open radio access network (O-RAN) rollouts.   $GAPW-B Gapwaves Gapwaves develops and fabricates specialized, millimeter-wave antennas utilizing its patented gap-waveguide technology. This low-loss, cost-efficient hardware is engineered to maximize the resolution, beam direction, and overall range of high-frequency radar systems without increasing power consumption. Gapwaves fits squarely into the autonomous systems and smart city landscapes by supplying Tier-1 automotive partners with the structural building blocks for next-generation ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems), enabling autonomous vehicles, industrial transport robots, and urban traffic monitors to build reliable, high-definition models of their surroundings under any weather condition. $SEYE Smart Eye Smart Eye makes computer-vision hardware and software platforms dedicated to Human Insight AI, tracking eye gaze, facial expressions, and head movements in real time. The company’s primary footprint is in the automotive sector, where its edge-based Driver Monitoring Systems (DMS) and interior cabin sensing modules identify operator distraction, drowsiness, and cognitive load to comply with global safety mandates. This technology bridges the human-machine interface across smart transportation and autonomous vehicles, while its subsidiaries (like Affectiva) extend this technology into emotion AI, which is increasingly utilized in behavioral research, aerospace simulators, and interactive robotics. $AMBA Ambarella Ambarella builds ultra-low-power, high-efficiency system-on-chip (SoC) vision and radar processors built on its algorithm-first CVflow artificial intelligence architecture. Its chips, like the 4nm CV7 and the high-compute N1 series, serve as the localized brain for Edge AI, industrial robotics, and smart city infrastructure by running complex convolutional and transformer neural networks simultaneously. By integrating advanced image signal processing, hardware-accelerated video encoding, and native neural network computation on a single die, Ambarella allows security cameras, aerial drones, and autonomous mobile robots (AMRs) to execute edge-based multi-sensor perception, visual odometry, and real-time SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) entirely disconnected from the cloud. $OUST Ouster Ouster designs and manufactures high-resolution 3D digital LiDAR sensors alongside integrated perception suites, following its strategic combination with Stereolabs' ZED camera technology. By embedding native color and precise spatial mapping directly onto its proprietary silicon architecture, Ouster creates an end-to-end sensing platform for Physical AI. This hardware acts as the primary sensory organ for humanoid robotics, warehouse automation, autonomous mining fleets, and smart city infrastructure (via its BlueCity traffic management software), delivering low-latency, resilient environmental data that allows machines to map, track, and safely navigate complex physical spaces. $AMZN Amazon Within the AI and robotics verticals, Amazon develops custom infrastructure silicon—such as its proprietary Trainium and Inferentia acceleration chips—while engineering vast, automated logistics systems for its global fulfillment network. The company is a pioneer in warehouse robotics, deploying fleets of fully autonomous mobile robots like Proteus and intelligent manipulation systems like Sparrow to streamline its supply chain via machine learning and reinforcement learning. On the edge, Amazon implements localized AI and natural language processing through consumer ecosystems like Alexa, Ring, and the Astro home robot, establishing a ubiquitous presence across cloud AI architecture, industrial automation, and smart home systems. $ELMT Elmet Group Elmet Group fabricates precision-engineered components and high-power radio frequency (RF) or microwave equipment, operating through its Critical Materials Components and Engineered Microwave Products divisions. Specializing in the custom manufacturing of refractory metals like pure tungsten and molybdenum, Elmet supplies the foundational, high-durability hardware components required for advanced semiconductor fabrication, aerospace systems, and defense electronics. It fits into the advanced hardware and AI supply chain by delivering the critical thermal management substrates, ion implantation shielding, and robust RF distribution modules necessary to manufacture and run high-energy compute chips. $TRT Trio-Tech International Trio-Tech provides semiconductor back-end testing services and manufactures heavy-duty reliability equipment, including customized burn-in boards, wet process stations, and environmental testing chambers. The company serves as a vital gatekeeper for advanced hardware reliability, validating that complex chips can withstand severe thermal and electrical stress without failing prior to commercial rollout. Driven by the massive ramp-up in next-generation computing architectures, Trio-Tech directly supports AI and smart automotive expansion by stress-testing high-performance GPUs, CPUs, and electric vehicle (EV) electronics across its key facilities in Southeast Asia. $HLIT Harmonic Harmonic produces virtualized broadband access networks and software-based cloud video delivery infrastructure, known for solutions like its VOS360 cloud platform and cOS applications. The company embeds AI into smart systems by leveraging Content-Aware Encoding (CAE) algorithms and dynamic ad-insertion technologies to process mass media streams at the network edge, squeezing bandwidth requirements by up to 50% without compromising visual quality. This positions Harmonic as a fundamental utility provider for edge systems and smart cities, optimizing the heavy data pipelines that distribute live television, ultra-high-definition streaming, and localized broadband connectivity at scale."}}}, "rest_id": "2064063206776254486", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "11009", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
For those just starting to follow $SIVE, here's a breakdown of their previous earnings report 

On the surface it looked rough.

But underneath it was a 2027 story. Quick breakdown

The "ugly":

Revenue 61.9m SEK, down 22% YoY
Gross margin caved to 6.8% (was 11.7%)
Adj EBITDA -13.8m, worse than last year
Cash down to 26.6m from 73.5m a year ago

They burned ~49m in operating cash in a single quarter. That’s the real headline.

It gets spicier. To stay funded they:

Raised ~125m SEK in April (more dilution, share count 269m to 311m YoY)
Took a $17m loan facility at 12% / 10.85%

That is expensive money. The balance sheet quality got worse this quarter.

But here’s why I’m still holding:

The revenue miss is timing, not lost business. North America sales halved purely from US defense budget approvals slipping into H2. Europe and Asia actually grew. Demand isn’t the problem.

The bull case is the pipeline:

Opportunity pipeline up 77% YTD to $799M

Jabil collab on a 1.6T pluggable transceiver (AI datacenter validation)
LIDAR auto ramp on track for Q4 2026
Multiple 2027 ramps stacking: FWA, defense, SATCOM

So the setup is simple. This quarter they got paid in pipeline and diluted in equity at the same time.

The whole thesis rides on the 2027 ramps actually converting to orders. If they slip the way Q1 revenue did, the cash position forces more dilution at worse terms

Now post earnings, we've gotten more validation. Still speculative but derisked. 

GFS deal: SIVE’s lasers are now the reference design inside GFS’s silicon photonics platform. So when GFS sells photonics chips to data center customers, SIVE rides along as the light source. Big validation, but no confirmed revenue yet.

Ayar x NVIDIA: Ayar’s CPO just got plugged into NVIDIA’s NVLink Fusion ecosystem. SIVE makes the lasers inside Ayar’s SuperNova product. So NVIDIA scales AI factories → Ayar ships more CPO modules → SIVE ships more lasers. Classic upstream pull-through.

text:
Yeah… I think all your upstream semi supply chain companies are going much higher.

Goldman now expects a combined $5.3 trillion of capex spending for the four largest hyperscalers $GOOGL / $META / MSFT / $AMZN from 2025 to 2030.

Revised up from $4.5T from Q1 earnings.

“Aggregate capex est. $7.6 trillion between 2026 and 2031.”

And it flows upward to these tiny chokepoints like $SIVE for CPO lasers/ $SOI for Silicon Photonics substrates. Leaderdrive/Harmonic for Humanoids components.

And so on…

Ai names don’t move in a straight line up,
but is just the beginning of the next Industrial Revolution as we move from  R&D/compute buildout into commercialization from Agents -> Physical AI -> discovery.

text:
$SIVE is my favorite CPO / photonics stock after AAOI.

Partly because it's Swedish and you have entertainment from comedians over there.

Today a new non-technical hedge fund called Protean Funds (likely shorting), went on air.

To said $SIVE CPO applications are imaginary.

Right after $GFS just made $SIVE their reference laser.

(Just for some context to newer readers: Lot of people in Sweden can only look at past 12 month revenue, and don't understand concepts of forward growth)

Also because they don't understand that no CPO application has scaled up yet at all.

So Swedish hedge funds keep going short (with many of their hedge funds like Colosseum / Origo heavily underwater).

But... for the technical readers... from H2 2026 to 2028, it goes from near $0 to $91B TAM in 1 1/2 years. (we're entering H2 now).

Overall TAM hits $141B (which is also 10x+ or so in 1 1/2 years)... and $SIVE has scaled into pluggable market with $JBL + other unnamed pluggable players with that too.

Probably not going to end well for the local Swedish firms, shorting right before the largest inflection points ever hits for $SIVE.

Just a matter of time before volume ramps.

text:
🚨 NEW TRADES ALERT

Rep. Gil Cisneros just disclosed up to $2,500,000 worth of new trades.

Cisneros sits on House Committee on Armed Services, a Navy veteran and former Under Secretary of Defense.

His latest disclosure includes buying the following military/defense related stocks within 3 weeks (May 4 - 29):

$THLEF - French defense electronics company, major NATO contractor in radars, missiles, naval combat systems, and satellite comms.

Post-Market Debrief

Crude premium persists as AI photonics and cybersecurity beat-and-raise offset microcap distress

Hormuz-driven crude tightness kept an inflation/rates premium, while AI-enabler tech and identity security printed beat-and-raise-style upside signals.

▪ Hormuz-linked crude constraint sustaining an inflation premium and reviving rates sensitivity
▪ AI infrastructure broadening into optical networking and silicon photonics with improved contract/backlog visibility
▪ Risk-capital retrenchment: dilution, going-concern and delisting headlines driving sharp microcap downside

Near-term direction remains headline-sensitive to crude: stabilization would likely extend the AI-enabler/cyber follow-through.

$CL=F

Not financial advice.

text:
Most people think the AI buildout is just a couple of stocks – Memory, CPU, GPU, NeoClouds, Photonics..

$SNDK, $MU, $ARM $INTC, $AMD, $NBIS, $LITE, $AAOI, $TSEM, $SIVE

The reality is there’s so much more to it.. I built a complete map of the AI buildout – at least the one I’ve been trading around for the last year.. I put that map on my Substack… 12 layers deep.

Since I’ve seen it circulating on here, I’ll just post it myself.

The hard part isn’t knowing the full stack – it’s making sense of it.

Why is photonics down today but memory isn’t? Why is CPO so relevant in the AI buildout?

Bullish AF on AI infrastructure.

Go check out the full article for free on my Substack, and if you’re going to share it – please do, but link back to my article.

Here is the link: https://t.co/AqKTdxnlmJ

Let’s go find the next 1000% stock ⚡️⚡️

text:
📢 𝐉𝐔𝐒𝐓 𝐈𝐍: $MU Micron Appoints Former NVIDIA Executive Alexis Black Björlin to Board - $NVDA $META $AVGO $INTC 

👉 𝐊𝐞𝐲 𝐇𝐢𝐠𝐡𝐥𝐢𝐠𝐡𝐭𝐬:

➤  Micron appoints Dr. 𝐀𝐥𝐞𝐱𝐢𝐬 𝐁𝐥𝐚𝐜𝐤 𝐁𝐣ö𝐫𝐥𝐢𝐧 to its board of directors.

➤  Björlin currently serves as 𝐂𝐡𝐢𝐞𝐟 𝐒𝐭𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐞𝐠𝐲 𝐎𝐟𝐟𝐢𝐜𝐞𝐫 at General Catalyst.

➤  Previously led 𝐃𝐆𝐗 𝐂𝐥𝐨𝐮𝐝 as senior vice president at NVIDIA.

➤  Brings expertise across 𝐀𝐈 𝐢𝐧𝐟𝐫𝐚𝐬𝐭𝐫𝐮𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐞, cloud, semiconductors, and networking.

➤  Held senior leadership roles at 𝐌𝐞𝐭𝐚, 𝐁𝐫𝐨𝐚𝐝𝐜𝐨𝐦, 𝐈𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐥, and Source Photonics.

➤  Serves on boards including 𝐃𝐢𝐠𝐢𝐭𝐚𝐥 𝐑𝐞𝐚𝐥𝐭𝐲, Astera Labs, and Celestial AI.

💬 𝐄𝐱𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐭 𝐒𝐭𝐚𝐭𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭:

“Alexis is a respected technology and business leader with exceptional expertise at the intersection of AI infrastructure, cloud systems and semiconductors,” said Sanjay Mehrotra, Chairman, President and CEO of Micron.

“Her experience leading large-scale technology platforms and advancing some of the industry’s most important innovations will be a tremendous asset to Micron. We are very pleased to welcome Alexis to our board and look forward to the insight she will bring as we continue to build on our technology leadership and capture the significant opportunities ahead.”

“I am delighted to welcome Alexis to the Micron board of directors,” said Lynn Dugle, Micron’s lead independent director.

“Alexis brings a rare combination of technical depth, strategic vision and operating experience across some of the world’s most important technology companies. I am confident she will contribute to our board’s work and to Micron’s long-term success.”

credit first: this one's real. a named, booked $8.2M production order (https://t.co/pcEEAe5HxP, Ka-band beamforming ICs) beats the "reference design / photonixFAB option" stuff this name usually trades on. take the win.
but the next line is the tell: "the bigger implication is not the contract size." when someone tells you to look past the number and feel the implication, they're selling the sequel, not the order. one $8.2M order isn't a defense-prime design-in that "leads to volume contracts" yet — that's the hoped-for next chapter written as done. and now it's a space/defense play and a SpaceX-IPO play and the AI-photonics story, all stacked on one micro-cap.
price the $8.2M that exists, not the ramp that's still a sentence. watch the filings for repeat orders. NFA. $SIVE
