text:
Marvell as the safest entry into the photonics buildout. Ouster + FieldAI for color lidar on mobile robots. Sivers' GF deal for 1.6-terabit lasers (the stock is up around 1,700% YTD).

That's @kaizen_investor's thesis, tracked on schwerelos. $MRVL +148%, OUST +178%, SIVE +185% YTD on the follow-returns. 30 scored calls, 66.67% win rate, #2 on the board.

4 stocks up 10%+ today in a sea of red that I’m bullish on long-term.

$BESS $VECO $FCEL $ADUR

Do your own DD, but I think all 4 attack unique bottlenecks most investors aren’t paying attention to.

Repost. Bookmark. Subscribe. Thank you all for the support. 

$BESS

Thinking out loud…

Everyone is focused on GPUs, but AI doesn’t work without power. Bimergen is developing roughly 2 GW across 23 battery storage projects and has talked about a path to $300M-$400M in annual revenue over the next 3-4 years. They recently advanced a 100 MW / 400 MWh Redbird project, acquired 79.2 MW of late-stage ERCOT assets, and partnered on an additional 480 MWh portfolio.

The niche here is grid storage. AI data centers are creating enormous electricity demand and storage is becoming increasingly important for grid stability, renewable integration, and power availability.

High risk, but the valuation disconnect is interesting.

$VECO

This might be my favorite of the group.

One of the biggest AI bottlenecks isn’t compute anymore—it’s moving data efficiently. Veeco recently announced more than $250M in equipment orders tied to Indium Phosphide laser production for 800G and 1.6T optical transceivers used in AI infrastructure.

FY26 revenue guidance is $740M-$800M and management expects compound semiconductor revenue to grow roughly 50% this year.

The niche is optical connectivity. AI clusters need photonics, silicon photonics, and advanced networking to connect thousands of accelerators together. That’s where Veeco’s equipment comes in.

$FCEL

The earnings numbers weren’t pretty.

Q2 revenue came in at $35.6M and the company posted a loss, but what caught my attention was the pipeline.

Management reported a 4 GW sales pipeline, up 267% sequentially, with roughly 90% tied to data center opportunities. They also launched a standardized 12.5 MW Energy Block and are expanding manufacturing capacity toward 500 MW annually.

The niche is data center power generation. Grid interconnection delays are becoming a major AI bottleneck and companies are looking for reliable behind-the-meter power solutions.

Risky, but definitely worth watching.

$ADUR

This one isn’t directly AI related, but it solves a different infrastructure bottleneck.

Aduro recently reported a 47-hour continuous pilot run with 86% liquid hydrocarbon yields and about 85% of output in the C20-and-below range suitable for existing naphtha cracker infrastructure.

Cash sits around $39M and the company is advancing its first commercial-scale facility in Europe.

The niche is industrial efficiency and feedstock processing. If successful, the technology could unlock value from waste streams that traditional recycling struggles to handle while integrating into existing petrochemical infrastructure.

Not every opportunity has to be AI. Sometimes the biggest winners solve overlooked bottlenecks in large industries.

Just some names I’m watching closely.

Repost. Bookmark. Subscribe. Thank you all for the support. $BESS Thinking out loud… Everyone is focused on GPUs, but AI doesn’t work without power. Bimergen is developing roughly 2 GW across 23 battery storage projects and has talked about a path to $300M-$400M in annual revenue over the next 3-4 years. They recently advanced a 100 MW / 400 MWh Redbird project, acquired 79.2 MW of late-stage ERCOT assets, and partnered on an additional 480 MWh portfolio. The niche here is grid storage. AI data centers are creating enormous electricity demand and storage is becoming increasingly important for grid stability, renewable integration, and power availability. High risk, but the valuation disconnect is interesting. $VECO This might be my favorite of the group. One of the biggest AI bottlenecks isn’t compute anymore—it’s moving data efficiently. Veeco recently announced more than $250M in equipment orders tied to Indium Phosphide laser production for 800G and 1.6T optical transceivers used in AI infrastructure. FY26 revenue guidance is $740M-$800M and management expects compound semiconductor revenue to grow roughly 50% this year. The niche is optical connectivity. AI clusters need photonics, silicon photonics, and advanced networking to connect thousands of accelerators together. That’s where Veeco’s equipment comes in. $FCEL The earnings numbers weren’t pretty. Q2 revenue came in at $35.6M and the company posted a loss, but what caught my attention was the pipeline. Management reported a 4 GW sales pipeline, up 267% sequentially, with roughly 90% tied to data center opportunities. They also launched a standardized 12.5 MW Energy Block and are expanding manufacturing capacity toward 500 MW annually. The niche is data center power generation. Grid interconnection delays are becoming a major AI bottleneck and companies are looking for reliable behind-the-meter power solutions. Risky, but definitely worth watching. $ADUR This one isn’t directly AI related, but it solves a different infrastructure bottleneck. Aduro recently reported a 47-hour continuous pilot run with 86% liquid hydrocarbon yields and about 85% of output in the C20-and-below range suitable for existing naphtha cracker infrastructure. Cash sits around $39M and the company is advancing its first commercial-scale facility in Europe. The niche is industrial efficiency and feedstock processing. If successful, the technology could unlock value from waste streams that traditional recycling struggles to handle while integrating into existing petrochemical infrastructure. Not every opportunity has to be AI. Sometimes the biggest winners solve overlooked bottlenecks in large industries. Just some names I’m watching closely. $TE $BE $EOSE $TYGO $FLNC $SHLS $NBIS $VELO $SILC $RDW $POET $AMBA $AMBQ $LODE $NVTS $DGXX $IREN $CRWV Not financial advice. Title: “Can Light Move a Machine?” Timestamp: June 9, 2026 Tagged: #Photonics #Robotics #Vulliez #DirectedEnergy #NorthstarLumen #WitnessScience #CanonicalLedger
⸻
⚖️ Core Proposition:
Can directed energy, light, or photonic systems cause robotics to move?
The answer is not science fiction.
It's already happening.
⸻
🧠 Forensic Findings:
Modern robotics routinely use:
• Lasers for navigation • LiDAR for environmental mapping • Optical sensors for positioning • Infrared guidance systems • Photonic communication networks • Machine vision driven by reflected light
Without light, many robotic systems become blind.
⸻
🔁 The Northstar+Lumen h-AI™ Effect-to-Cause™ Model
Effect:
A robotic system moves.
Cause:
The machine receives information.
That information may arrive through: • Radio frequency • Fiber optics • Cameras • Laser guidance • Infrared signals • Structured light projections
The machine is not responding to "light" alone.
It is responding to information carried by light.
⸻
🛰️ Why This Matters
Photonics is becoming one of the most important fields in robotics, autonomy, defense systems, manufacturing, transportation, and sensing technologies.
As sensors improve, machines increasingly interpret the world through optical inputs.
The question is no longer:
"Can light move a machine?"
The question is:
"How much of the machine's behavior depends upon what the light tells it to see?"

Like in this robotic raccoon that was positioned near a whistleblower’s former home? 
⸻
🧭 Northstar Observation
The future battlefield, factory floor, smart city, and autonomous vehicle all share a common dependency:
Light becomes data. Data becomes decisions. Decisions become motion.

The headline is the trillion-dollar tag. 

Disaggregate the compute across the data center and connectivity becomes the product. Marvell is the architecture layer for that. Closer to Broadcom than Lumentum, exactly like you framed it.

What most are missing: this was not a free compliment. Nvidia put $2B into Marvell on March 31 through NVLink Fusion, with a silicon photonics pledge attached. Jensen was talking his own book on stage.

text:
EU Chips Act 1 was about supply security.

Chips Act 2.0 looks more like market-building: demand accelerators, procurement, faster permits, strategic projects, AI/cloud/data-centre pull-through, and deeper support for photonics, advanced packaging, MEMS, specialty manufacturing and materials.

That maps well to parts of my EU semi basket:

$XFAB → specialty foundry / industrial chips / advanced packaging / SiPh-adjacent capacity
$SIVE → optical interconnects, lasers, AI data-centre photonics
$SILEX → MEMS foundry / sensors / specialty manufacturing
$RIB → MBE equipment for compound semis / photonics materials
$SMOL → more speculative, via advanced packaging / ultra-thin capacitor IP

None of this guarantees company-level success.

But the policy backdrop is becoming more favourable, especially for regional semiconductor ecosystems as geopolitics pushes supply-chain resilience higher on the agenda.

Having said that each company still has to execute: convert strategic relevance into customers, orders, margins, capacity, partnerships and cash flow.

Let's see how the future pans out but for now it seems bullish.

Chips Act 2.0 looks more like market-building: demand accelerators, procurement, faster permits, strategic projects, AI/cloud/data-centre pull-through, and deeper support for photonics, advanced packaging, MEMS, specialty manufacturing and materials. That maps well to parts of my EU semi basket: $XFAB → specialty foundry / industrial chips / advanced packaging / SiPh-adjacent capacity $SIVE → optical interconnects, lasers, AI data-centre photonics $SILEX → MEMS foundry / sensors / specialty manufacturing $RIB → MBE equipment for compound semis / photonics materials $SMOL → more speculative, via advanced packaging / ultra-thin capacitor IP None of this guarantees company-level success. But the policy backdrop is becoming more favourable, especially for regional semiconductor ecosystems as geopolitics pushes supply-chain resilience higher on the agenda. Having said that each company still has to execute: convert strategic relevance into customers, orders, margins, capacity, partnerships and cash flow. Let's see how the future pans out but for now it seems bullish."}}}, "rest_id": "2062260142737883321", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "606", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}
